title: Plan the Showcase
developmental_frame: This is the year's capstone, and Think leads it. At 9–10, a child can genuinely plan a small real event if it's broken into steps (Q3), helped along with clear decisions (Q2), and grounded in noticing what's needed (Q1). Owning the plan for a real Showcase Day — a small at-home event where they show and sell what they've made this year — is a huge, proud milestone. Keep the scope small and the child genuinely in charge.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is being the *planner* of a real event that actually happens — the whole year's thinking skills put to work on something that matters. It's the leap from doing small tasks to organising a whole day, and being trusted to do it.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child is put genuinely in charge of planning a real event that their family will attend. Being trusted to run the plan — making the decisions, owning the list, setting the date — is enormously motivating at this age. The event is real, the responsibility is real, and that pulls their best thinking out of them.

artifact: A written plan for Showcase Day: what the day will include, a list of everything needed, a step-ladder of things to do (with who and when), and the key decisions made — pulling together noticing, deciding, and step-planning from the whole year.
success_criteria: The child decided what Showcase Day will include and can explain their choices.
They listed what's needed and broke the preparation into ordered steps.
They presented the plan and a real date was set.

lessons.title: What's the showcase?
lessons.concept: First, decide what your Showcase Day will be: a small event where you show and share the things you've made this year, with a little stall to sell some. Use your deciding skills to choose what to include — you can't do everything, so pick the best bits.
lessons.real_example: Your showcase might be: a table showing your inventions and digital creation, a little stall selling your bookmarks, and a five-minute 'tour' you give your family. Small, real, and doable in an afternoon.
lessons.apply_task: Decide what your Showcase Day will include. Write a short list of the 3–4 things that will happen.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Did you pick things you're proud of and that are doable at home? What did you leave out, and why?
lessons.feedback_task: Share your showcase idea with a parent and check it's the right size — not too big, not too small.
lessons.teach_back_task: Explain to a sibling what your Showcase Day will be and why you chose those parts.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is your day, built from your year — you decide what goes in it. That's yours, not a computer's.
success_criteria: A short list of what Showcase Day will include is decided.

lessons.title: Notice what's needed
lessons.concept: Use your noticing skills to spot everything the day needs — a table, chairs, signs, snacks, money box, your things to show. Missing one thing can cause a wobble on the day, so list it all now.
lessons.real_example: A stall needs: a table, a sign with prices, your things laid out, a box for money, and a chair. Noticing all of it beforehand means no panic on the day.
lessons.apply_task: Walk through your Showcase Day in your mind and list everything you'll need for each part.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which thing would be easiest to forget? Those are exactly the ones a good list catches.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your 'needed' list to a parent and ask what you've missed.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a family member list what's needed for something *they're* planning.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Picturing your real day at your real home is something only you can do. Walk through it in your own mind.
success_criteria: A checklist of everything needed for the day exists.

lessons.title: Make the step ladder
lessons.concept: Now use your step-by-step skill to turn the whole plan into ordered steps: what to do this week, what to do the day before, what to do on the morning. A big event is just a step ladder, same as any tricky task.
lessons.real_example: This week: make the goods and the sign. Day before: set up the table, make snacks. Morning of: lay out the stall, put up signs, invite everyone in. One rung at a time.
lessons.apply_task: Break your whole plan into a step ladder, in order, with roughly when each step happens.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Looking at your ladder, is anything left too late? Better to spot it now than the morning of.
lessons.feedback_task: Show your step ladder to a parent and agree which steps they might help with.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show someone how a whole event becomes a simple step ladder.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Build your own ladder — sequencing the steps is the thinking. An adult can help with timing since they know the household's week.
success_criteria: The preparation is broken into an ordered, timed step ladder.

lessons.title: Share the plan and set the date
lessons.concept: A plan isn't real until people agree to it. Present your whole plan to your family, get their buy-in, and set a real date. Now Showcase Day is actually happening — and the other three parts (inviting, making, selling) can begin.
lessons.real_example: You show your family the plan, they say 'great — let's do it two Saturdays from now', and suddenly it's real. That date is what turns a plan into an event.
lessons.apply_task: Present your full plan to your family and agree on a real date for Showcase Day. Write the date at the top of your plan.
lessons.reflection_prompt: How did it feel to present a whole plan you made? What questions did your family ask?
lessons.feedback_task: Ask your family if the plan makes sense and if the date works for everyone.
lessons.teach_back_task: Tell someone the whole plan for your Showcase Day as if inviting them to help.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is a real family conversation to agree a real date — a very human, in-person moment. Present it yourself, proudly.
success_criteria: The plan was presented to the family.
A real date for Showcase Day was set.
